Name: pac_bayes_scaling
Title: PAC-Bayes Framework for Scaling Dynamics in Machine Learning with Spectral Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, we can develop a novel approach for scaling dynamics in machine learning that provides rigorous guarantees on the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.
Related Work: Our work builds upon recent advances in PAC-Bayes theory and spectral stability bounds. While existing works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have focused on adaptive mixed-precision training and quantization, our approach explicitly integrates PAC-Bayes frameworks with spectral stability bounds to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference. This distinguishes our work from existing literature, which often relies on heuristic-based methods or manual configurations.
Abstract: We propose a novel PAC-Bayes framework for scaling dynamics in machine learning, leveraging spectral stability bounds to derive rigorous guarantees on the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency. Our approach integrates implicit test-time optimization and explicit complementary pathways to provide a flexible and modular framework for scaling machine learning models. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through extensive experiments on benchmark datasets, showcasing significant improvements in accuracy and efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate the effectiveness of our PAC-Bayes framework on benchmark datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10) using various machine learning models (e.g., ResNet, ViT).
Experiment 2: Investigate the impact of spectral stability bounds on the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.
Experiment 3: Compare our approach with state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., SNIP, AMP-ViT, EMQ) in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and scalability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Theoretical guarantees may not always translate to empirical performance.
Computational overhead of spectral stability bounds may be significant.
Limited applicability to certain types of machine learning models or datasets.
